title: drizzle-orm 0.39.x has high-severity SQL-injection advisory (GHSA via improperly escaped SQL identifiers)
description: `npm audit` reports drizzle-orm (pinned at 0.39.3 in CLAUDE.md tech-stack, resolved in the lockfile) carries a HIGH advisory: 'Drizzle ORM has SQL injection via improperly escaped SQL identifiers.' This is a server-runtime dependency that ships in the Railway Express bundle (`server/storage.ts` uses Drizzle for every DB query), so the advisory is in the actually-deployed runtime, not just build tooling. The fix is `drizzle-orm@0.45.2`, flagged isSemVerMajor=true, so `npm audit fix` will not apply it without `--force`; it needs a deliberate, tested major upgrade. Exposure depends on whether any column/table identifier is ever attacker-influenced â€” Opus uses static schema identifiers, so practical exploitability is likely low, but the advisory is real and unpatched.
evidence: npm audit --json: drizzle-orm [high] fixAvailable={"name":"drizzle-orm","version":"0.45.2","isSemVerMajor":true} via=["Drizzle ORM has SQL injection via improperly escaped SQL identifiers"]
suggestedFix: Plan a tested drizzle-orm 0.39 -> 0.45 major upgrade (run the server __tests__ + drizzle-kit push against a scratch DB). Confirm no dynamic identifier interpolation exists in server/storage.ts before relying on the low-exploitability assumption.

title: episodeStorage.ts has no test file â€” concurrency mutex, status-transition guard, and 7-day linger filter all untested
description: episodeStorage.ts (322 lines) has zero test coverage. A grep across client/lib/__tests__/ for any of its exports (allocateEpisodeSequence, getVisibleDashboardEpisodes, updateEpisode, EPISODE_STATUS_TRANSITIONS) returns nothing, and no test file imports from episodeStorage. The two existing episode-named test files cover different modules (episodeHelpers.test.ts â†’ episodeHelpers.ts suggestEpisodeType/suggestEpisodeTitle; burnsEpisode.test.ts â†’ burnsEpisodeHelpers.ts). The module contains non-trivial decision logic that is cheap to test by mocking AsyncStorage + encryption (the pattern existing storage tests already use): (1) allocateEpisodeSequence (line 119) â€” the atomic read-increment-persist mutex documented in CLAUDE.md as replacing the racey getCasesByEpisodeId(id).length+1 pattern; it serialises concurrent allocations and returns 1 for a missing episode; this is a clinical-attribution-relevant counter (episodeSequence stamped on cases) and its concurrency invariant is entirely unverified. (2) updateEpisode (line 159) â€” enforces EPISODE_STATUS_TRANSITIONS, dropping only an illegal status field while still persisting other updates (e.g. completedâ†’planned rejected but co-submitted metadata still saved). (3) getVisibleDashboardEpisodes (line 256) â€” filters completed episodes by a 7-day linger window using parseIsoDateValue on resolvedDate, excludes cancelled, keeps active/on_hold/planned. All three are deterministic given mocked storage and have clear pass/fail behaviour.
evidence: grep -rl for allocateEpisodeSequence|getVisibleDashboardEpisodes|updateEpisode|EPISODE_STATUS_TRANSITIONS across client/lib/__tests__/ returns no matches; episodeHelpers.test.ts imports from @/lib/episodeHelpers, burnsEpisode.test.ts imports from ../burnsEpisodeHelpers â€” neither touches episodeStorage.ts. episodeStorage.ts:119 `export async function allocateEpisodeSequence`, :159 `export async function updateEpisode` with EPISODE_STATUS_TRANSITIONS guard, :256 `export async function getVisibleDashboardEpisodes` with 7-day SEVEN_DAYS_MS linger filter.
suggestedFix: Add client/lib/__tests__/episodeStorage.test.ts. Mock @react-native-async-storage/async-storage (in-memory Map), ./encryption (encryptData/decryptData as identity passthrough), ./storage (hashPatientIdentifier, getCasesByEpisodeId), and ./activeUser (userScopedAsyncKey). Cover: allocateEpisodeSequence returns 1 for a missing episode, increments nextCaseSequence across sequential calls, and serialises two concurrent (un-awaited) calls into distinct sequence numbers (the mutex invariant); updateEpisode rejects an illegal status transition (e.g. completedâ†’planned) while still persisting a co-submitted non-status field, and applies a legal transition; getVisibleDashboardEpisodes includes active/on_hold/planned, excludes cancelled, includes a completed episode resolved within 7 days, and excludes one resolved >7 days ago or with no resolvedDate.

title: Server-runtime moderate advisories (ws, qs/express, ip-address/express-rate-limit) have non-breaking fixes available
description: `npm audit --omit=dev` surfaces several moderate advisories in dependencies that ship to the Railway server bundle and all report `fixAvailable: true` (non-major): `ws` (GHSA-58qx-3vcg-4xpx uninitialized memory disclosure), `qs` (remotely-triggerable DoS in qs.stringify, reached transitively through `express`), and `ip-address` (reached through `express-rate-limit`). express + express-rate-limit are core security middleware per CLAUDE.md (helmet@8 migration commit 944b580). These are all patchable with a plain `npm audit fix` (no breaking change), unlike the drizzle-orm one.
evidence: npm audit --json: ws [moderate] fixAvailable=true 'Uninitialized memory disclosure'; qs [moderate] fixAvailable=true 'remotely triggerable DoS: qs.stringify crashes'; express-rate-limit [moderate] via ip-address fixAvailable=true. Metadata: {moderate:21, high:3, critical:0} on --omit=dev.
suggestedFix: Run `npm audit fix` (non-force) to bump ws/qs/ip-address transitives, then re-run `npm ci --include=dev`, `npm run check:types`, and `npm run test:coverage` to confirm CI stays green before committing the updated lockfile.

title: Unused export deleteEpisode
description: `deleteEpisode(id)` is exported but has zero importers anywhere in the repo (client/server/shared, including tests). Episode removal is only ever done via `clearAllEpisodes`. A repo-wide grep for `deleteEpisode` excluding the definition file returns no hits.
evidence: grep -rn 'deleteEpisode' across client/server/shared (excluding episodeStorage.ts) -> no output. Definition at episodeStorage.ts:189 `export async function deleteEpisode(id: string)`.
suggestedFix: Delete the `deleteEpisode` function (lines 189-200), or keep it only if a deletion UI is planned.

title: Unused export getActiveEpisodes
description: `getActiveEpisodes()` is exported but has zero importers anywhere in the repo. Dashboard episode visibility is served by `getVisibleDashboardEpisodes()` (used in useActiveEpisodes.ts) instead, which supersedes this function. A repo-wide grep for `getActiveEpisodes` excluding the definition file returns no hits.
evidence: grep -rn 'getActiveEpisodes' across client/server/shared (excluding episodeStorage.ts) -> no output. Definition at episodeStorage.ts:217 `export async function getActiveEpisodes()`.
suggestedFix: Delete the `getActiveEpisodes` function (lines 217-235); `getVisibleDashboardEpisodes` is the live equivalent.

title: No-op queueMicrotask cleanup block in allocateEpisodeSequence
description: The `finally` block schedules a `queueMicrotask` whose entire body is a comment-only no-op: the `if` condition is evaluated (`_episodeSequenceLocks.get(episodeId) === prior.then(() => thisLock)` â€” which is always false since `prior.then(...)` allocates a fresh Promise each call) and the block does nothing. This is unreachable/dead logic that allocates a throwaway Promise on every call and never cleans up the lock map entry it claims to.
evidence: Lines 150-155: `queueMicrotask(() => { if (_episodeSequenceLocks.get(episodeId) === prior.then(() => thisLock)) { /* No-op ... */ } });` â€” body is only a comment; `prior.then(() => thisLock)` creates a new Promise so the equality is always false.
suggestedFix: Remove the `queueMicrotask(...)` block (lines 150-155) entirely; it has no effect. If lock-map cleanup is actually desired, replace with a real deletion guarded by a captured reference to the chain stored in the map.

title: Dead no-op queueMicrotask block; _episodeSequenceLocks map never pruned
description: The `finally` block in `allocateEpisodeSequence` contains a `queueMicrotask` whose entire body is a comment-only no-op: the `if` condition is computed but does nothing inside it (just two comment lines). The intended map cleanup is never performed â€” entries in the module-level `_episodeSequenceLocks` Map are only ever replaced by the next call for the same episodeId (line 127-130), never deleted. Over an app session this means one permanently-resolved promise chain accumulates per distinct episodeId allocated against. The chains are tiny and resolved (GC reclaims the closure bodies), but the Map keys/entries themselves persist for the process lifetime. The block is dead code that reads as if it does cleanup but doesn't â€” a maintenance trap. Either delete the queueMicrotask entirely (the comment already concedes the next call replaces the entry) or actually delete the map entry when this is the last waiter.
suggestedFix: Remove the queueMicrotask no-op block entirely (it does nothing), OR implement real cleanup: in the finally, after release(), check if the current map value is still this lock's chain and `_episodeSequenceLocks.delete(episodeId)` if so. Simplest: delete the dead block since the existing replace-on-next-call behaviour bounds growth to the number of distinct episode IDs touched per session.

title: @xmldom/xmldom high advisories present but confined to Expo build tooling (not shipped in app binary)
description: `npm audit --omit=dev` reports 3 HIGH advisories rooted in `@xmldom/xmldom` (XML injection / DoS via unsafe CDATA, DocumentType, processing-instruction and comment serialization), reached through `@bacons/xcode` -> `@expo/plist` and `@bacons/apple-targets` (the iOS native-targets toolchain). These appear under --omit=dev only because `expo` and `@bacons/apple-targets` are declared as runtime `dependencies` rather than devDependencies in this repo, but @xmldom/xmldom is exercised only during prebuild/native-target generation on the dev/EAS-build host â€” it does not execute inside the shipped iOS binary or the Railway server. Real-world risk is build-host XML parsing of project-controlled plist input, i.e. low. Worth tracking but not a runtime exposure.
evidence: npm audit --omit=dev --json: @xmldom/xmldom [high] via 5 XML-injection/DoS advisories; chain @bacons/xcode -> @expo/plist -> @xmldom/xmldom. Also @bacons/apple-targets, @expo/cli, @expo/prebuild-config in the --omit=dev set (build tooling declared as deps).
suggestedFix: Track via `npm audit`; a fix lands when @bacons/apple-targets / @expo bumps the transitive @xmldom/xmldom. No action needed for runtime security. Optionally reclassify pure build tooling (@bacons/apple-targets) into devDependencies so --omit=dev audit reflects true runtime surface.

title: Dead no-op queueMicrotask block in allocateEpisodeSequence finally
description: The `finally` block in `allocateEpisodeSequence` schedules a `queueMicrotask` whose body is an empty no-op: the `if` condition compares the map entry against a freshly-constructed `prior.then(() => thisLock)` promise (a brand-new Promise object that can never reference-equal the value stored in `_episodeSequenceLocks`), and even when entered the block does nothing but contain comments. It is purely dead code â€” the lock is already released via `release()` on the line above, and the map entry is overwritten by the next allocator call as the comment itself states. This is harmless at runtime but is misleading: it reads as if it performs cleanup when it does not, and the `_episodeSequenceLocks` map entries are never actually removed (a slow unbounded-by-episode-id leak, though bounded in practice by the number of distinct episodes). Confined to this in-scope file.
suggestedFix: Delete the `queueMicrotask(...)` block entirely (lines 150-155); keep only `release()` in the finally. If genuine map cleanup is desired, store the lock promise in a local variable and compare against that exact reference before deleting the map key.
